This week’s luxury news for founders, creatives, and entrepreneurs: Visit the link in our bio for the complete briefing. Mercedes-Maybach (mercedesmaybach) is planning a 500-foot gigayacht, but it’s not for charter. The ‘Maybach Ocean Club’ is a co-ownership model with just 30 suites for 300 members, essentially creating a floating private residence. A new definition of nautical luxury, landing in 2029. Meanwhile, in New York, Le Alfré (@le.alfre) is reimagining retail. The brand has opened a 6,000 sq ft “Penthouse” in Midtown, accessible by private elevator and reservation only, where clients can browse the collection over a martini. It’s an unhurried, high-touch model. Saudi Arabia (@visitsaudi) is getting into opulent train travel. The “Dream of the Desert” is a newly unveiled locomotive with 33 private suites, set to travel a 1,300km route from Riyadh and offering a new, climate-controlled way to see the Kingdom. And in wellness, the legendary Austrian clinic Lanserhof (@lanserhof) has secured a $110 million boost. The new funding will fuel a significant global expansion, pushing the science-backed longevity brand into new territories with a Spanish location and a beauty line. Members can access all stories, headlines, and updates 48 hours early via the link in our bio. Follow @thelibermag if you’re a founder, creative, or entrepreneur. #theliber #maybach #oceanclub #gigayacht #yachtlife #lealfre #experientialretail #menswear #nyc #dreamofthedesert #saudiarabia #luxurytrain #luxurytravel #lanserhof #longevity #wellness #health #lifestyle #luxury #travel #entrepreneur

“This is the first synthetic species,” microbiologist J. Craig Venter told 60 Minutes’ Steve Kroft in 2010. His team of scientists created a synthetic bacteria designed on a computer, with man-made DNA. Venter says that from concept to completion, it took 15 years and $40 million to make. Venter, who died this week at 79, was one of the most famous scientists in the world, known for his pioneering work in deciphering the human genetic code.

The people we admire most didn’t get there by avoiding failure — they got there by walking through it. Every setback, rejection, and bad call taught them something they couldn’t have learned any other way. Failure is one of life’s greatest teachers, if you let it be.
